JavaScript Core (42) — Functions and How This Works: call, apply, bind, and Strict Mode
JavaScript Core
Functions and How This Works

This time we get into some of the more interesting corners of this — namely call, apply, and bind. These three look almost identical, which is exactly why people mix them up. I'll walk through how each one redirects this, where they differ, and then finish with what strict mode actually does for this.

JavaScript Core (41) — Functions and This: The Simple Call
Functions and This

This one picks up where the last chapter left off and covers the "Simple Call" — what it is, the situations that trigger it, and why it always sends `this` flying off to `window`. We'll walk through IIFEs, closures, callbacks like setTimeout and forEach, and the classic trick of stashing `this` in a variable so it doesn't get lost.

JavaScript Core (40) — Functions and How This Works: The Most Common this, Object Method Calls

Time to tackle the thing that trips up so many JavaScript developers — this. In this post I start with the most common case: calling a method on an object. We'll see where this comes from, why it points where it points, and how it can quietly change depending on how you call the function.
/en/javascript/2020/12/22/how-to-know-image-loaded/
How Do You Know When an Image Has Finished Loading?
Image

Sometimes you need to know whether the images on a page have finished loading — maybe to show a loading state, or to swap in a fallback when an image 404s. This post records how I handle exactly that, using new Image() and its load event and complete property.

JavaScript Core Concepts (39) - Functions and This: Leveling Up Closures with the Factory Pattern and Private Methods

Now that we understand the basics of closures, it's time to push them a little further. This post covers two slightly more advanced uses: the factory pattern, which lets you spin up new closures from user-supplied arguments, and private methods, where a closure returns an object full of methods instead of a single function.
/en/javascript/2020/12/20/javascript-core-38-closure/
JavaScript Core Concepts (38): Closures, and Why They're Really About Memory

Closures are a big deal in JavaScript, and the reason comes down to one thing: memory. In this post we'll see how a closure keeps a variable alive, why that's tied to garbage collection, and how you can use it to build your own private, self-contained state.
/en/javascript/2020/11/29/javascript-core-37-functions-this-parameters/
JavaScript Core Concepts (37) — Functions and This: Parameters

Parameters are one of those concepts that quietly run everything once you start working with functions. In this post we'll dig into how arguments map to parameters, what the weird array-like `arguments` object actually is, what happens when a parameter name collides with a variable inside the function, and how passing objects vs. values changes everything thanks to pass-by-reference.
/en/javascript/2020/11/18/javascript-core-36-functions-this-iife/
JavaScript Core Concepts (36): Functions & This — The IIFE

The IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expression) is one of those tricks you see everywhere — in libraries, in frameworks, in old codebases. This post walks through what an IIFE actually is, how to write one, how it keeps your variables from leaking into the global scope, and the one tiny semicolon gotcha that trips everyone up.

JavaScript Core (35) - Functions and How This Works - What Is a Function?

A brand new chapter kicks off here — and this one comes with the notorious this. Before we get there, let's nail down what a function actually is in JavaScript, what makes it special, and the difference between function expressions and function declarations.

JavaScript Core Concepts (34): Objects — The Quiz Round
JavaScript Core Concepts
Object

We've covered a lot of object stuff, so now it's time for the quiz. Ten questions covering array-of-objects access, reference vs value, JSON formatting, functions as objects, and shallow vs deep copy. Read the code, pick your answer, then check whether you got it.
